8. Generalization Theory (Omar Khattab)

  1. Intuitive ideas about generalization
  2. Some classical ideas in generalization theory
  3. What do these ideas tell us (or fail to tell us!) about deep learning
  4. Deep nets generalize, but often in unpredictable ways
  5. Why do deep nets generalize? → What are their inductive biases?
  6. What might a useful theory for deep net generalization look like?
  • Assessing model complexity is an open question in deep learning.

8.1. VC Theory

  • Define generalization error as population error minus training error:

    (18)

  • Informally, Vapnik-Chervonenkis theory says that if size of training set >> size of function class, then training error matches population error with high probability
  • Neural networks are able memorize the dataset / fit noise (Zhang 2017), e.g. if you randomly assign labels to images. This implies that the training error does NOT match population error. However they still generalize well. VC bounds are sufficient but not necessary, so this is okay. Why do deep nets generalize well?

  • Even polynomials also can show double descent. See and below.

    We believe this is because optimization implicitly picks the one with the smallest coefficient norm: Among all interpolating polynomials, choose the one with the smallest coefficient norm in the given basis.

  • The simple + spiky hypothesis says that the learned model is simple + spiky as we overparameterize more and more.
  • We conclude that the classical bias-variance picture only describes the underparameterizedside.

8.2. Deep nets

  • DNNs seem to even generalize compositionally. If we train on lazy data, e.g. “make a haiku” and the label is a trivial haiku. Then the neural net will also reward hack in new settings. Studied for AI safety.

  • Reversal curse (Berglund 2025): models train on “A is B” do not automatically infer “B is A”.

  • We believe that deep learning must have some nice inductive biases. Ideas:

    1. Architectural symmetries

    2. Simplicity bias in the parameter-function map: if we randomly initialize the weights, we usually get a simple function.

    3. Optimizer bias: SGD converges to “flat” minima, not those that are spiky. Also: weight decay (ridge penalty) acts as an regularizer on weights as well as initialization near zero.
    4. Our collective search for good hyperparmeters and design choices: activation functions, layer norm, etc.
    5. Per project hyperparameter tuning and using a validation set.
    6. Starting with pre-trained networks (foundation models)

8.3. Modern theory

  • The learned function space is not uniform. We should have a prior on certain parts.

  • Countable hypothesis bound (PAC Bayes): the population risk is the empirical risk plus some complexity penalty that is small when you are very confident in your learned model class (this is improved by inductive biases).

  • Philosophical: maybe deep learning itself is a good prior that can’t be described English.
